title: Compaq Vows Support for Himalaya Post-HP (Securities Industry News)
author: Maria Trombly (Securities Industry News)
abstract: Securities Industry News (April 2002) by Maria Trombly. Final results of the HP-Compaq merger vote not yet in but early indications show approval. Compaq working overtime to ease customer concerns about Himalaya integration: 95% of the world's securities transactions go through Compaq Himalaya mainframes (formerly Tandem); 106 of the 120 world stock exchanges. Customers include J.D. Edwards, Prudential Securities, T.D. Waterhouse, and Nasdaq Stock Market. Compaq spokesman Arch Currid: 'We've already assured all of our customers, on every product front, that they can count on our ongoing long-term support.' Counter-warnings from analysts. Lance Travis (AMR Research): 'HP can certainly stand there and say yes... but who knows what will happen?... HP doesn't have anything directly to replace the Himalaya, [but] there is a risk that it will get shoved aside and starved to death. I think it behooves people to start looking for alternatives.' Quote from Peter Kastner of Aberdeen Group on customer impact: top-500 combined customers should continue to see very high levels of sales/support; but customers below that 500 line who were previously highly placed in the smaller company's pecking order may see less support — fewer direct sales force visits, possible loss of onsite sales/support office, account-team reshuffling. Kastner recommends IT organizations at major financial firms hold information-exchange meetings so newly assigned account staff can come up to speed on present and future needs.
importance_rationale: Captures Aberdeen/Kastner's analyst voice on the most consequential 2002 tech-industry M&A: HP-Compaq, which absorbed Tandem (NonStop Himalaya) and DEC. Particular relevance to financial-services IT given the 95% securities-transaction share. Kastner's tactical advice (information exchange meetings, account-team transition planning) has memoir value as practitioner-class guidance from an analyst who had been Stratus marketing in the 1980s.
relevance_rationale: NonStop Himalaya line still ships in 2025 (HPE NonStop). The post-merger customer-support concerns Kastner raised remain canonical in M&A integration literature: top-tier customers preserved, mid-tier customers absorb the cost. Financial-services dependence on fault-tolerant transaction systems has only deepened.
prescience_rationale: Kastner correctly predicted (a) HP would preserve the high-volume Himalaya business — vindicated; HPE NonStop continues to operate exchanges in 2025 — and (b) sub-top-500 customers would experience integration friction. AMR Travis's worry that HP would 'starve' Himalaya did not materialize: HP/HPE invested in the line. Kastner's middle path (preserve top, friction below) was the more accurate forecast.
